title: How interest rates actually work
subtitle: Interest rates are not just the price of debt. They are a signal about economic conditions, central bank policy, and the entire structure of asset prices.

body.content: An interest rate is the price of borrowing money over time. Simple concept. But interest rates don't just affect your mortgage or credit card — they set the price of everything in the financial system. When the Fed raises rates, stock valuations fall, bond prices drop, housing gets more expensive, and businesses slow hiring. One number, affecting everything. Worth understanding.
What an interest rate is actually made of
Any interest rate you see is a combination of several components baked into one number. Understanding the components tells you why rates are where they are and where they might go.

items: Risk-free rate: the baseline — what you'd earn lending to the safest borrower with no risk of default (US Treasury bills approximate this)
Inflation premium: compensation for the fact that the dollars you get back will buy less than today's dollars
Credit spread: extra yield for lending to a riskier borrower who might not pay you back
Liquidity premium: extra yield for lending in a form that's hard to exit quickly

body.label: Key concept
body.content: A 7% credit card rate vs. a 24% credit card rate: the difference is almost entirely credit risk and profit margin. The lender charges you more because the statistical likelihood of non-repayment on revolving consumer credit is much higher than on a secured mortgage. Every interest rate you see tells a story about how the lender assesses the borrower.
The Fed and why it matters

The Federal Reserve sets the federal funds rate — the rate at which banks lend to each other overnight. This is the anchor rate for the entire US financial system. When the Fed raises this rate, borrowing becomes more expensive everywhere: mortgages, car loans, business lines of credit, student loans. When the Fed cuts it, borrowing gets cheaper and economic activity tends to pick up.

From March 2022 to July 2023, the Fed raised rates from near 0% to 5.25% — the fastest rate increase in 40 years. The direct effects: mortgage rates doubled from ~3% to ~7%, bond prices fell significantly, and tech stocks (which are valued on future earnings) got hit hard. All from one policy variable.

This one surprises people. Stocks are not bonds — why would interest rates matter for stock valuations? Because the value of any asset is the present value of its future cash flows. The rate used to discount those future cash flows is tied to prevailing interest rates. When rates rise, future earnings are worth less today. A company expected to earn $100 million in 10 years is worth less when discounted at 6% than at 2%.

body.label: The math
body.content: $100 million received in 10 years: discounted at 2% = $82 million today. Discounted at 6% = $56 million today. That's a 32% difference in present value from a 4% change in rates. This is why growth stocks (which earn most of their cash far in the future) are particularly sensitive to rate changes. Their valuations are long-duration assets, just like long-duration bonds.

The yield curve plots interest rates across different maturities for US Treasury bonds — from 3-month bills to 30-year bonds. Normally it slopes upward: you get paid more for lending longer, because longer commitments carry more uncertainty. An inverted yield curve — where short-term rates are higher than long-term rates — is unusual, and has preceded every US recession in the past 50 years. Not a guarantee, but a serious signal worth knowing about.

Interest rates are the most important variable in the financial system. They set the price of debt, determine the discount rate for all future cash flows, and ripple through stocks, bonds, real estate, and the broader economy. When rates change, everything changes. Pay attention to them.
